issues.original: Every time you ask Claude a question, autocomplete a sentence in Gmail, or read a Google Translate page, you are touching a stack that took seventy years to assemble. Natural Language Processing is the discipline that taught machines to read, score, transform, and write human language — and the surprising thing is how much of the modern stack still rests on a small set of preprocessing primitives invented decades ago.
issues.fixed: Every time you ask Claude a question, autocomplete a sentence in Gmail, or read a Google Translate page, you're using a stack that took seventy years to build. Natural Language Processing (NLP) is the field that taught machines to read, score, transform, and write human language. Surprisingly, much of the modern NLP stack still relies on preprocessing techniques invented decades ago.

issues.original: This first article in the series does two things. First, it draws the map: where the field came from, what it covers today, and why the tools we use look the way they do. Second, it builds the foundational layer — cleaning, tokenization, normalization, and feature extraction — with code that you can lift directly into a project. By the end you will have a reusable preprocessing pipeline and, more importantly, a sense of when each step helps and when it quietly destroys signal.
issues.fixed: This first article in the series does two things. First, it maps out the field's history, current scope, and the reasons behind the tools we use. Second, it builds the foundational layer — cleaning, tokenization, normalization, and feature extraction — with code you can directly use in a project. By the end, you'll have a reusable preprocessing pipeline and, more importantly, an understanding of when each step is helpful and when it can destroy signal.

issues.original: The 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' replaced recurrence with self-attention: Two practical consequences mattered. First, the model is fully parallel across positions, so training scales with GPU memory rather than sequence length. Second, every token can attend directly to every other token, which finally solved the long-range dependency problem. BERT, GPT, and every modern LLM are direct descendants.
issues.fixed: The 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' replaced recurrence with self-attention. Two practical consequences followed. First, the model is fully parallel across positions, so training scales with GPU memory rather than sequence length. Second, every token can attend directly to every other token, solving the long-range dependency problem. BERT, GPT, and all modern LLMs are direct descendants.

issues.original: The same model can produce a sharp answer or a confident hallucination. The difference is rarely the weights — it is the framing. A vague request like *"analyze this text"* gets you a generic summary; a prompt with a role, two clean examples, and a strict output schema gets you something a parser can consume. **Prompt engineering is the discipline of turning that gap into a repeatable system instead of a lucky shot.**
issues.fixed: The same model can produce a sharp answer or a confident hallucination. The difference lies in the framing, not the weights. A vague request like *"analyze this text"* yields a generic summary; a prompt with a role, two clear examples, and a strict output schema produces something a parser can use. **Prompt engineering turns that gap into a repeatable system rather than a lucky shot.**

issues.original: In-Context Learning (ICL) is the mechanism that makes this work. When you put a few examples inside the prompt, the model does not retrain; it conditions its forward pass on those examples and effectively *infers a task* from them. Understanding what ICL can and cannot do is the difference between a developer who fights the model and one who steers it.
issues.fixed: In-Context Learning (ICL) is the mechanism that makes this work. When you include a few examples in the prompt, the model doesn't retrain; it conditions its forward pass on those examples and effectively *infers a task* from them. Understanding ICL's capabilities and limitations distinguishes a developer who struggles with the model from one who guides it.

issues.original: This part is the seventh in the NLP series. It assumes you know roughly how a Transformer decoder generates tokens (Part 4) and what an autoregressive LM is (Part 6). Everything below is grounded in published behavior — but be warned: the literature on prompt engineering is unusually noisy, and most numbers are model- and dataset-specific. Treat the bars in the figures as illustrative shapes, not benchmark claims.
issues.fixed: This is the seventh part in the NLP series. It assumes you have a basic understanding of how a Transformer decoder generates tokens (Part 4) and what an autoregressive LM is (Part 6). Everything below is based on published behavior — but be warned: the literature on prompt engineering is unusually noisy, and most numbers are specific to models and datasets. Treat the bars in the figures as illustrative, not benchmark claims.

issues.original: A prompt is *a single text string the model conditions on*. Everything else — "system" vs. "user" roles, function schemas, retrieval results — is just structured text the API stitches into one sequence before tokenization. Treating a prompt as a flat string with named blocks is the cleanest mental model.
issues.fixed: A prompt is *a single text string the model conditions on*. Everything else — such as "system" vs. "user" roles, function schemas, and retrieval results — is just structured text the API combines into one sequence before tokenization. Treating a prompt as a flat string with named blocks provides the cleanest mental model.

issues.original: The five blocks below are not mandatory, but most production prompts contain a subset of them, in roughly this order:
issues.fixed: The five blocks below are not mandatory, but most production prompts include a subset of them, in roughly this order:

issues.original:
A pragmatic prompt builder:

Two design notes that beginners often miss:

- **Order matters.** Examples placed *after* the format spec but *before* the query consistently work better than examples buried at the top — recency biases the decoder.
- **Stable prefix, variable suffix.** Put everything that does not change (system, examples, format) at the top so KV-cache reuse and prompt-cache features can work. Variable input goes last.
issues.fixed:
A pragmatic prompt builder:

Two design notes that beginners often miss:

- **Order matters.** Examples placed *after* the format spec but *before* the query work better than examples at the top — recency biases the decoder.
- **Stable prefix, variable suffix.** Place all static elements (system, examples, format) at the top to enable KV-cache reuse and prompt-cache features. Variable input goes last.

issues.original:
These are the principles I would still teach today, after a lot of prompts in production:

1. **Clarity over cleverness.** Replace "analyze the text" with "classify the text into {positive, negative, neutral} and return JSON". You are competing with every plausible interpretation the model could pick up.
2. **Specificity buys determinism.** Say what *not* to do, and what to output when uncertain ("if you cannot answer from the document, return [CODE]"). Models honor negative constraints surprisingly well.
3. **Context completeness.** If the answer needs a definition the model could plausibly not have, include it. Cheaper than a wrong answer.
4. **Role assignment when relevant.** "You are a senior security reviewer" measurably narrows the distribution of outputs on code-review tasks. It is *not* a magic spell — avoid it for generic tasks where it just adds tokens.
issues.fixed:
These are the principles I would still teach today, after working with many prompts in production:

1. **Clarity over cleverness.** Replace "analyze the text" with "classify the text into {positive, negative, neutral} and return JSON". You are competing with every plausible interpretation the model could pick up.
2. **Specificity buys determinism.** Specify what *not* to do and what to output when uncertain ("if you cannot answer from the document, return [CODE]"). Models honor negative constraints surprisingly well.
3. **Context completeness.** Include any definitions the model might need. This is cheaper than a wrong answer.
4. **Role assignment when relevant.** Assigning a role, like "you are a senior security reviewer," measurably narrows the output distribution for code-review tasks. Avoid it for generic tasks where it just adds tokens.

issues.original: A frozen language model is a confident liar. It cannot read yesterday's incident report, your company wiki, or the patch notes that shipped this morning, so when you ask, it confabulates an answer that is grammatically perfect and factually wrong. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** breaks the deadlock by separating *memory* from *reasoning*: keep the LLM small and stable, and put the volatile knowledge in an external store that you can update at any time. Before generating, retrieve the relevant evidence and condition the model on it.
issues.fixed: A frozen language model is a confident liar. It can't read yesterday's incident report, your company wiki, or the patch notes that shipped this morning, so when you ask, it confabulates an answer that is grammatically perfect but factually wrong. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** breaks the deadlock by separating *memory* from *reasoning*: keep the LLM small and stable, and put the volatile knowledge in an external store that you can update anytime. Before generating, retrieve the relevant evidence and condition the model on it.

issues.original: The idea is one paragraph. The engineering is the rest of this article. A real RAG system has roughly a dozen knobs — chunk size, embedding model, index type, [FORMULA], hybrid weighting, reranker depth, prompt template, citation format, refusal policy — and most of them interact. We walk through each one with the math, the trade-offs, and code that runs.
issues.fixed: The idea is one paragraph; the engineering is the rest of this article. A real RAG system has about a dozen knobs — chunk size, embedding model, index type, [FORMULA], hybrid weighting, reranker depth, prompt template, citation format, refusal policy — and most of them interact. We walk through each one, covering the math, trade-offs, and running code.

issues.original: In practice we approximate the sum two ways. The cheap and dominant approach (**stuff** in LangChain terms) concatenates the top-[FORMULA] documents into the LLM context and lets attention do the marginalisation implicitly. The principled but expensive approach (**Fusion-in-Decoder**, used in Atlas and the original RAG paper) encodes each [FORMULA] pair separately and fuses in the decoder. For most production systems, stuff with [FORMULA] and a good reranker is the right answer.
issues.fixed: In practice, we approximate the sum in two ways. The cheap and dominant approach (**stuff** in LangChain terms) concatenates the top-[FORMULA] documents into the LLM context, letting attention handle the marginalization implicitly. The principled but expensive approach (**Fusion-in-Decoder**, used in Atlas and the original RAG paper) encodes each [FORMULA] pair separately and fuses them in the decoder. For most production systems, using stuff with [FORMULA] and a good reranker is the right choice.

issues.original: This factorisation is what makes RAG attractive: - **Retriever** ([FORMULA]) — cheap to update. Reindex a document set in minutes; no GPU needed at inference. - **Generator** ([FORMULA]) — frozen. The same LLM serves every domain. - **Citations** — every claim traces back to a chunk. You can show it to a user, audit it, or use it to filter hallucinations.
issues.fixed: This factorization is what makes RAG attractive: - **Retriever** ([FORMULA]) — cheap to update. Reindex a document set in minutes; no GPU needed for inference. - **Generator** ([FORMULA]) — frozen. The same LLM serves every domain. - **Citations** — every claim traces back to a chunk. You can show it to a user, audit it, or use it to filter hallucinations.

issues.original: Three details earn their keep. The **separator hierarchy** in the splitter keeps paragraphs and sentences whole. The **[CODE]** removes one source of variance so retrieval becomes the only knob. The **refusal clause** in the system prompt is the cheapest hallucination guard you have.
issues.fixed: Three details are crucial. The **separator hierarchy** in the splitter keeps paragraphs and sentences intact. The **[CODE]** removes one source of variance, making retrieval the only knob. The **refusal clause** in the system prompt is the cheapest hallucination guard.

issues.original: For decades, machines treated "king" and "queen" as unrelated symbols — nothing more than two distinct slots in a vocabulary list. Then a single idea changed everything: what if every word lived in a continuous space, and meaning was just a *direction*? Once that idea took hold, models could compute
issues.fixed: For decades, machines treated "king" and "queen" as unrelated symbols—nothing more than two distinct slots in a vocabulary list. Then a single idea changed everything: what if every word lived in a continuous space, and meaning was just a *direction*? Once that idea took hold, models could compute

issues.original: and the entire trajectory of NLP turned toward representation learning. This article walks through that turn — from the failure of one-hot vectors, to Word2Vec's shallow networks, to the global statistics that GloVe exploits, to the subword n-grams that let FastText handle words it has never seen — and finally connects embeddings to the language models that gave rise to them.
issues.fixed: and the entire trajectory of NLP shifted toward representation learning. This article walks through that shift—from the failure of one-hot vectors, to Word2Vec's shallow networks, to the global statistics that GloVe exploits, to the subword n-grams that let FastText handle unseen words—and finally connects embeddings to the language models that gave rise to them.

issues.original: Given a vocabulary of [FORMULA] words, one-hot encoding maps each word to a [FORMULA]-dimensional indicator vector: Three properties make this representation a dead end:
issues.fixed: Given a vocabulary of [FORMULA] words, one-hot encoding maps each word to a [FORMULA]-dimensional indicator vector. Three properties make this representation a dead end:

issues.original: - **Sparsity.** With [FORMULA], every vector is 99.998% zeros. Storage and compute are wasted on emptiness.
issues.fixed: - **Sparsity.** With [FORMULA], every vector is 99.998% zeros, wasting storage and compute on emptiness.

issues.original: - **No similarity.** The dot product between any two distinct one-hot vectors is exactly zero. The model literally cannot tell that "cat" is closer to "dog" than to "quantum"; from its point of view, all non-identical words are equally different.
issues.fixed: - **No similarity.** The dot product between any two distinct one-hot vectors is exactly zero. The model can't tell that "cat" is closer to "dog" than to "quantum"; from its perspective, all non-identical words are equally different.

issues.original: - **No generalisation.** A linear classifier on top of one-hot input has [FORMULA] independent weights per class. Knowing that "movie" is positive teaches the model nothing about "film".
issues.fixed: - **No generalization.** A linear classifier on top of one-hot input has [FORMULA] independent weights per class. Knowing that "movie" is positive doesn't teach the model anything about "film".

issues.original: A word embedding maps each word to a dense vector of [FORMULA] dimensions (typically 100--300): Now [FORMULA], parameters are shared across semantically related words, and downstream models suddenly need orders of magnitude fewer examples to generalise. The challenge becomes: where do the numbers in those vectors come from?
issues.fixed: A word embedding maps each word to a dense vector of [FORMULA] dimensions (typically 100--300). Now, parameters are shared across semantically related words, and downstream models need orders of magnitude fewer examples to generalize. The challenge is: where do the numbers in these vectors come from?

issues.original: Every embedding method in this article rests on one observation, attributed to the linguist J. R. Firth: **"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."** Concretely, two words that habitually appear in similar contexts probably have similar meanings.
issues.fixed: Every embedding method in this article is based on an observation by the linguist J. R. Firth: **"You shall know a word by the company it keeps."** Specifically, two words that often appear in similar contexts likely have similar meanings.

issues.original: If we can train any model to predict context from a word — or vice versa — the parameters that succeed at this prediction task will have absorbed distributional regularities. The embeddings emerge as a *byproduct* of the prediction.
issues.fixed: If we can train a model to predict context from a word—or vice versa—the parameters that succeed at this task will have absorbed distributional regularities. The embeddings emerge as a *byproduct* of the prediction.

issues.original: The payoff of taking this hypothesis seriously is shown below: trained embeddings encode meaningful relations as nearly constant directions in space, which is why the famous analogy arithmetic works.
issues.fixed: The payoff of taking this hypothesis seriously is shown below: trained embeddings encode meaningful relations as nearly constant directions in space, which is why the famous analogy arithmetic works.
